subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Observational Learning and Child Development (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study investigated whether the presence of a peer model influences the willingness of 4-year-old children to try unfamiliar foods.
Participants
Ninety-six children between the ages of 3 years 10 months and 4 years 6 months were recruited from six preschool programs in a suburban school district. Written parental consent was obtained for all participants. Children with known food allergies or developmental delays were excluded from participation, resulting in a final sample of 88 children (46 girls, 42 boys). Families were predominantly middle-income; 61% of families identified as White/European American, 18% as Hispanic/Latino, 12% as Asian/Asian American, 6% as Black/African American, and 3% as Other.
Method
Children were individually escorted to a small testing room adjacent to their classroom. In the room was a small table with two place settings. Each setting included four small cups, each containing approximately 5 grams of a different novel food item (jicama, starfruit, dried mango, and edamame). These foods were selected because pretesting confirmed that fewer than 10% of the target age group had previously encountered them.
Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions:
• Peer model condition: The child sat across from a confederate child of the same gender (a trained 5-year-old from a different classroom) who enthusiastically tasted all four foods before the participant was prompted to try them.
• No model condition: The child sat alone at the table and was simply asked if they would like to taste any of the foods.
In both conditions, the experimenter offered no encouragement or praise. After three minutes, the experimenter recorded how many of the four food items the child had tasted (score of 0–4) and how many grams total the child had consumed (measured via a digital scale). A follow-up measure three weeks later used parent diaries to record whether children had voluntarily requested any of the four novel foods at home.
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that children exposed to a peer model would try more novel foods than children in the no-model condition. The results are summarized in the table below.

Mean Scores by Condition

| Measure | Peer Model Condition | No Model Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Number of foods tasted (out of 4) | 3.1 | 1.4 |
| Grams consumed (mean) | 8.7 | 3.2 |
| Parent diary: requested novel food at home (% yes) | 54% | 21% |

Children in the peer model condition tasted significantly more foods, consumed larger quantities, and were more than twice as likely to later request those foods at home. The researchers concluded that peer modeling is a powerful mechanism for shaping food neophobia in early childhood and that the effects may persist beyond the laboratory setting.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately identifies the research method as an experiment.

required_elements: This was an experiment because children were randomly assigned to the peer model or no model condition.
alternatives.correct_example: This was an experiment because children were randomly assigned to the peer model or no model condition.

required_elements: The researchers conducted an experiment by manipulating the independent variable (presence or absence of a peer model) and randomly assigning participants to conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers conducted an experiment by manipulating the independent variable (presence or absence of a peer model) and randomly assigning participants to conditions.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of willingness to try novel foods as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response states that willingness to try novel foods was operationally defined as the number of food items tasted (out of 4) and/or the total grams of food consumed, as measured after the three-minute testing period.

required_elements: The operational definition is the number of novel food items a child tasted out of four options during the three-minute session.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition is the number of novel food items a child tasted out of four options during the three-minute session.

required_elements: Willingness to try new foods was operationalized as the number of foods tasted and total grams consumed, recorded by the experimenter after the session.
alternatives.correct_example: Willingness to try new foods was operationalized as the number of foods tasted and total grams consumed, recorded by the experimenter after the session.

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the difference in grams consumed between the two conditions.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately describes that children in the peer model condition consumed a greater mean amount of food (8.7 grams) compared to those in the no model condition (3.2 grams), a difference of 5.5 grams.

required_elements: On average, children who observed a peer model ate about 2.7 times more food than those without a model.
alternatives.correct_example: On average, children who observed a peer model ate about 2.7 times more food than those without a model.

required_elements: The peer model condition produced higher food consumption than the no model condition, with means of 8.7 and 3.2 grams respectively.
alternatives.correct_example: The peer model condition produced higher food consumption than the no model condition, with means of 8.7 and 3.2 grams respectively.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: The response identifies that parental informed consent was obtained for all child participants.

required_elements: The response identifies that children with food allergies were excluded, protecting them from potential physical harm (beneficence/protection from harm).
alternatives.correct_example: The response identifies that children with food allergies were excluded, protecting them from potential physical harm (beneficence/protection from harm).

required_elements: The response identifies that deception regarding the confederate child could require debriefing of parents/participants after the study.
alternatives.correct_example: The response identifies that deception regarding the confederate child could require debriefing of parents/participants after the study.

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students assert the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a reason it is generalizable and a reason it is not.

scoring_points.description: The response proposes a claim about generalizability referencing a relevant population, with evidence from the participant information.

required_elements: The findings may not be generalizable beyond 4-year-old suburban middle-income children because the sample was drawn from only six preschool programs in a single school district.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may not be generalizable beyond 4-year-old suburban middle-income children because the sample was drawn from only six preschool programs in a single school district.

required_elements: The findings may be limited in generalizability because the sample was predominantly White/European American (61%), which may not represent the full population of preschool-aged children.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may be limited in generalizability because the sample was predominantly White/European American (61%), which may not represent the full population of preschool-aged children.

required_elements: The study may generalize to young children of similar age and developmental stage because the sample was narrowly defined and controlled for age range.
alternatives.correct_example: The study may generalize to young children of similar age and developmental stage because the sample was narrowly defined and controlled for age range.

parts.prompt: Explain how Bandura's concept of vicarious reinforcement could account for why children in the peer model condition tasted more foods.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately explains that vicarious reinforcement occurs when someone learns by observing another person being rewarded (or not punished) for a behavior, and that children in the peer model condition observed the model enthusiastically tasting foods without negative consequences, which increased the children's own willingness to try the foods.

required_elements: Children learned through vicarious reinforcement: they observed the peer model tasting new foods with apparent enjoyment and no negative outcome, which encouraged them to try the foods themselves.
alternatives.correct_example: Children learned through vicarious reinforcement: they observed the peer model tasting new foods with apparent enjoyment and no negative outcome, which encouraged them to try the foods themselves.

required_elements: Bandura's vicarious reinforcement explains the findings because the children saw the model receive what appeared to be a pleasant eating experience, reinforcing their own approach behavior toward the foods.
alternatives.correct_example: Bandura's vicarious reinforcement explains the findings because the children saw the model receive what appeared to be a pleasant eating experience, reinforcing their own approach behavior toward the foods.

parts.prompt: Explain how Vygotsky's concept of the zone of proximal development (ZPD) could relate to how the peer model influenced the children's behavior.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately explains that the ZPD is the range of tasks a learner cannot accomplish independently but can accomplish with the guidance or modeling of a more capable peer, and that the peer model (slightly older, more capable in this context) helped children expand their behavior beyond what they would have done alone.

required_elements: The peer model served as a more capable peer who helped children in their ZPD — trying unfamiliar foods was something the children could do with a model's guidance, even if they would not have done it independently.
alternatives.correct_example: The peer model served as a more capable peer who helped children in their ZPD — trying unfamiliar foods was something the children could do with a model's guidance, even if they would not have done it independently.

required_elements: Vygotsky's ZPD applies because the presence of the peer model provided scaffolding that helped the children try new foods they would not have tried on their own.
alternatives.correct_example: Vygotsky's ZPD applies because the presence of the peer model provided scaffolding that helped the children try new foods they would not have tried on their own.
